variables.content: Within the conventional Landau Fermi-liquid framework for a three-dimensional Fermi liquid, the standard mapping that relates the molar specific-heat coefficient \gamma to the quasiparticle effective-mass renormalization m^{*}/m and that yields the Landau parameter F_{1} from the effective mass is assumed to be valid and numerically accurate to within the quoted experimental uncertainties; applying these standard relations to the experimentally determined \gamma=2.11\ \mathrm{K}^{-1} gives m^{*}/m\approx 2.12 and F_{1}\approx 3.36.

In the low-temperature limit the normal-liquid $^3$He data exhibit Fermi-liquid linearity,
      $S(T,P)=C_P(T,P)=\gamma(P)\,R\,T\quad\text{as }T\to0$,
      where $R$ is the molar gas constant and $\gamma(P)$ is the molar linear coefficient; from the self-consistent numerical data $\gamma(P)$ and its pressure derivative $\gamma'(P)$ were extracted across the pressure range by linear extrapolation of $C_P/T$ to $T=0$. The extracted $\gamma(P)$ was used to obtain the quasiparticle effective-mass ratio via
      $\dfrac{\gamma(P)}{\gamma_0}=\dfrac{m^*}{m}=1+\dfrac{1}{3}F_1^s$,
      where the reference coefficient for a noninteracting Fermi gas at the same density is
      $\displaystyle \gamma_0=8.991\times10^{-2}\ \mathrm{K}^{-1}\left(\frac{V(0,P)}{\mathrm{cm}^3}\right)^{2/3}$
      (with $m$ the atomic mass of $^3$He), so that the Landau parameter $F_1^s$ follows from $m^*/m=\gamma/\gamma_0$; the computed $\gamma(P)$ increases with pressure over the studied range and the corresponding derived Fermi-liquid parameters were obtained consistently from these $\gamma(P)$ values.

Let $p_{F}$ denote the Fermi momentum, $m$ the bare particle mass, and $m^{*}(ρ)$ the effective mass as a function of density derived from low-temperature specific-heat data; define the Landau parameters $F_{0}(ρ)$ and $F_{1}(ρ)$ and adopt the ground-state Fermi-liquid relation
      $c^{2}=\dfrac{p_{F}^{2}}{3m^{2}}\dfrac{1+F_{0}}{1+\tfrac{1}{3}F_{1}},$
      together with $F_{1}=3\big(m^{*}/m-1\big)$. Using the density dependence of $m^{*}$ inferred from low-temperature specific-heat measurements and the fitted sound speed $c(ρ)$, the parameter $F_{1}(ρ)$ is represented as the power series in $x=(ρ-ρ_{0})/ρ_{0}$
      $F_{1}(ρ)=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{1}x+\alpha_{2}x^{2}+\alpha_{3}x^{3}+\cdots,$
      with empirical coefficients $\alpha_{0}=5.6$, $\alpha_{1}=30$, $\alpha_{2}=-50$, $\alpha_{3}=65$. Substituting these results and the appropriate $p_{F}$ and $m$ into the Fermi-liquid relation yields a power-series expansion for $F_{0}(ρ)$,
      $F_{0}(ρ)=\beta_{0}+\beta_{1}x+\beta_{2}x^{2}+\beta_{3}x^{3}+\cdots,$
      with empirical coefficients $\beta_{0}=9.6$, $\beta_{1}=88$, $\beta_{2}=175$, $\beta_{3}=-35$. These series provide empirical density dependences for the Landau parameters in liquid 3He within the density interval spanned by the input data.

Assuming the standard Landau Fermi-liquid relation that the low-temperature linear specific-heat coefficient \gamma is proportional to the quasiparticle density of states at the Fermi surface (hence proportional to the quasiparticle effective mass m^{*}), the measured value \gamma=2.11\ \mathrm{K}^{-1} implies an effective-mass ratio m^{*}/m\approx 2.12 (where m is the bare fermion mass) and, under the usual formulas that relate the effective mass renormalization to the first Landau Fermi-liquid parameter, yields F_{1}\approx 3.36.

The proposition is that Landau Fermi-liquid theory applies to normal-liquid 3He in the studied pressure range in the $T\to0$ limit, so that the low-temperature specific-heat coefficient $\gamma(P)$, the quasiparticle effective mass $m^*(P)$, and the dimensionless Fermi-liquid parameter $F_1^s(P)$ are related by
      $$
      \frac{\gamma(P)}{\gamma_0}=\frac{m^*(P)}{m}=1+\frac{1}{3}F_1^s(P),
      $$
      where $m$ is the bare atomic mass of 3He and the reference coefficient $\gamma_0$ for a noninteracting Fermi gas at the same density is
      $$
      \gamma_0=\frac{\pi^2 k_B m}{\hbar^2}\left(\frac{V(0,P)}{3\pi^2 N}\right)^{2/3}.
      $$
      In this proposition $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $\hbar$ the reduced Planck constant, $N$ Avogadro's number, and the relation is assumed valid in the $T\to0$ Fermi-liquid limit so that $m^*/m$ and $F_1^s(P)$ can be extracted from the computed $\gamma(P)$ and $V(0,P)$ by the algebraic inversion shown.
